Renaissance Early Modern

Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Medieval Latin Scholasticism

Nicole Oresme

c. 1320 – 1382

Late scholastic psychology, intellect, and anti-Averroist hylomorphism.

Nicole Oresme does not write a single treatise called “On Mind,” and the modern expression “philosophy of mind” has to be used with caution. His vocabulary is that of late medieval Aristotelian psychology and natural philosophy: anima, intellectus, potentiae or virtutes, species, phantasmata, sensus interiores, imaginatio, aestimativa, cogitativa, voluntas, and so on. Still, a systematic philosophy of mind can be extracted from his work. It is not a Cartesian philosophy of two complete substances, nor a simple theological doctrine of the soul, nor a reductive naturalism. It is a layered theory of mindedness: material sensitive souls in animals; a human being composed by both a material sensitive form and an immaterial intellective form; celestial intelligences or angelic minds as separate created intellects; and God as the uncreated, eternal, universal causal and moral limit of mind.

The central thesis I would extract is this: Oresme’s philosophy of mind is a dual-form, hylomorphic, anti-Averroist, anti-reductive account of human intellect, joined to a surprisingly naturalistic and quasi-constructivist account of perception, imagination, error, mental disorder, marvels, and animal cognition. The mind is embodied in its operations, especially through sense and imagination, but the human intellect is not reducible to bodily organs or material configurations. God and celestial intelligences are not detachable “theological distractions”; they are limit-cases that clarify the kinds of dependence a mind may have: dependence on matter, dependence on phantasms, dependence on a celestial sphere, dependence on divine creative causality, and dependence on divine justice.

1. Corpus and method

The most important source for Oresme’s psychology is his Expositio et quaestiones in Aristotelis De anima, especially Book III, questions 3–7, which Jack Zupko describes as a compact sequence on the metaphysical status of the human soul and intellect: whether the intellect is separate and unmixed, whether it inheres as a form, whether there is an agent intellect, whether the soul is eternal or perpetual, and whether there is one intellect for all human beings. Book II, question 5 is also crucial because it contains Oresme’s distinctive doctrine that the human being has two substantial forms: a material sensitive form and an immaterial intellective form.

The broader philosophy of mind must also include the Questiones super Physicam, the disputed question on the appearance of a thing, De causis mirabilium, De visione stellarum, De configurationibus qualitatum et motuum, the anti-divinatory writings, and Oresme’s vernacular Aristotelian translations and commentaries. The Stanford bibliography lists, among others, editions or translations of the Livre de divinacions, Tractatus contra astronomos, De configurationibus, Le livre du ciel et du monde, De visione stellarum, and Questiones super Physicam; Roudaut’s work also cites the modern editions of Oresme’s Quaestiones in De anima and other Aristotelian question-commentaries.

The most useful non-Anglophone interpretive strands I found are French and Spanish. French scholarship, especially Jean Celeyrette on appearances and imagination, treats Oresme’s theory of perception as an epistemology of fallible appearances, internal sense, and mathematical imagination. Spanish-language scholarship on Oresme’s De configurationibus draws attention to “spiritual configuration,” interior vision, and the moral formation of imagination. These are important because they prevent an overly narrow Anglophone reconstruction focused only on the soul’s ontology or on Oresme’s scientific naturalism.

2. The basic ontology: not one mind, but levels of minded being

Oresme’s psychology begins from the Aristotelian idea that soul is the form of a living body. But he modifies the standard hylomorphic picture in two directions. In non-human animals, the soul is deeply embodied, extended, and internally differentiated by localized powers. In human beings, there is not only a material sensitive soul but also an immaterial intellective soul. The result is not a single smooth continuum from plant to animal to human. It is a stratified metaphysics of cognitive life.

For non-human animals, Oresme’s view is close to a sophisticated materialism or emergentism, though those labels are modern and should be used carefully. Roudaut argues that Oresme rejects a strong Augustinian holenmerism for non-human souls: he does not think every power of the animal soul is virtually present in every part of the body. Instead, powers such as seeing, hearing, smelling, and touching are tied to determinate bodily parts or organs. Some sensitive powers may extend through large portions of the body, but powers are not all everywhere in the same way.

Recent work goes further. A 2026 article by Sylvain Roudaut, Henrik Lagerlund, and Erik Åkerlund argues that Oresme identifies soul and powers in such a way that the sensitive soul is “nothing but” a heterogeneous composition of distinct substantial parts; outside the human case, this blurs the distinction between body and soul and reduces higher-order psychological powers to lower physical powers.

This is important for philosophy of mind because Oresme is not simply a “spiritualist.” He has a robust theory of bodily, organ-localized, materially configured cognition. Animal mindedness is real, but it is the mindedness of an extended sensitive form, not of an immaterial intellect. This allows him to acknowledge complex perception, appetite, memory-like functions, learned dispositions, and even quasi-rational-seeming animal behavior without granting animals a human-style intellect.

The human case is different. Oresme holds that a human being has a material sensitive form comparable to the form found in a horse or donkey, and also an intellective form that is not material. The material form is generated from the potency of matter and is corruptible; the intellective soul is not educed from matter, is unextended, and is not corrupted by the body’s death.

This produces Oresme’s distinctive dualism. Zupko’s formulation is useful: Oresme posits an immaterial, unextended form that thinks and a material, extended sensitive form that senses, and these together constitute one substance, the human being. Oresme is therefore a dualist about forms, not a Cartesian dualist about two complete substances accidentally joined. The human being is still a per se unity, but the unity is achieved by the coordination of two metaphysically different forms.

Oresme’s position can be summarized like this:

Level Ontological status Cognitive profile Plant life Material living form Nutrition, growth, reproduction Animal mind Material, extended sensitive soul Sensation, imagination-like internal processing, appetite, locomotion Human mind Material sensitive form plus immaterial intellective form Sensation and imagination, plus universal thought, reflection, free intellectual agency Celestial intelligences / angelic minds Separate immaterial intellects associated with celestial spheres Intellectual activity and causal governance without ordinary bodily inherence God Uncreated eternal source and universal cause Not a faculty psychology, but the supreme metaphysical, causal, epistemic, and moral horizon

This hierarchy matters because Oresme’s account of human mind is neither reducible downward to animal sensation nor assimilable upward to angelic separation. The human intellect is immaterial, but it thinks as the intellect of an embodied human being.

3. The human soul: two substantial forms and one human being

Oresme’s doctrine of two human forms is the backbone of his philosophy of mind. In Book II, question 5 of the De anima commentary, he says that in a human being there is a single material form like that in a horse or donkey, and at the same time an intellective form that is not material. The donkey’s soul cannot remain after death, but the human intellective soul can. French-language exposition of this doctrine emphasizes exactly this: the human has an animal-type soul and also a separable intellective soul “of another nature,” capable of understanding, knowing, and knowing itself.

The philosophical pressure behind this position is clear. If there were only one immaterial soul in the human being, then bodily operations such as seeing, hearing, smelling, digestion, locomotion, and organic sensation would have to belong directly to an immaterial form. That would make embodied cognition too spiritualized. But if there were only a material soul, then universal thought, self-reflection, freedom, and immortality would become unintelligible. Oresme solves the problem by assigning bodily-sensitive life to a material form and intellectual life to an immaterial form.

The sensitive soul is extended because it is tied to extended organs and bodily parts. The intellective soul is not extended; it is indivisible and “whole” in the body, not by occupying every part as a spread-out quantity, but by a definitive presence. Oresme distinguishes this from circumscriptive presence, the way a body occupies a place by having part outside part. The intellective soul is spatially present only per accidens, through the body it informs.

This distinction between kinds of presence is not a scholastic technicality. It lets Oresme say that the intellect is genuinely in the human being, not a detached Averroist intellect merely connected from outside, while also denying that the intellect is physically spread through flesh and organs. The human intellect inheres in the person, but not as an extended material part. The sensitive soul is in organs materially and regionally; the intellective soul is in the body formally and definitively.

This also explains why Oresme rejects both Alexander of Aphrodisias and Averroes. Against Alexander, he refuses to reduce the human intellect to a material power generated from matter. Against Averroes, he refuses to make the human intellect a separate, common, non-inhering intellect shared by all human beings. Oresme’s middle way is: the intellect is immaterial, but it is the substantial form of an individual human body.

The result is a psychologically rich but metaphysically difficult view. A human being is one subject with two substantial forms. The material sensitive form disposes the body for the immaterial intellective form; the intellective form gives the human being rationality and personal intellectual life. Oresme acknowledges analogies with cases in which more than one intelligence may be associated with one celestial sphere, but he is cautious: analogies with celestial intelligences illuminate the possibility of multiple formal principles, but they do not erase the special unity problem of the human person.

4. Why Oresme thinks the human intellect is immaterial

Oresme gives a battery of arguments for the immateriality of the human intellect. Zupko lists ten major considerations in Oresme’s De anima III.3: the intellect cognizes material forms without being material; it has no corporeal organ; it is not fatigued as bodily powers are; it is not weakened by excessively intense objects in the way senses are; it can reflect upon itself; it can act independently in certain cases such as dreams or prophetic experiences; it acts when it wishes; it grasps universals; it debates and considers; and it is not determined by celestial influences.

These arguments do not function as modern deductive proofs. Oresme is often careful to present them as persuasive or plausible rather than demonstrative. That caution is philosophically important. He is not claiming that natural reason can produce an airtight proof of everything Christian doctrine teaches about the soul. But he does think that the best natural-philosophical explanation of human cognition requires an immaterial intellect.

The arguments can be grouped into four philosophical families.

First, there are intentionality arguments. The intellect can think universals, necessary relations, eternal truths, and separate substances. A purely material power tied to a determinate organ seems unable to become intentionally universal in this way.

Second, there are reflexivity arguments. The intellect can think itself and its own acts. Sense powers are directed outward or toward determinate bodily functions; intellect has a self-referential capacity that suggests a different metaphysical status.

Third, there are organ-independence arguments. Sight needs eyes, hearing needs ears, smell needs the appropriate organ, and so forth. Oresme thinks intellect has no single corporeal organ in that sense. It uses phantasms and sensory materials, but it is not the act of a determinate organ.

Fourth, there are freedom arguments. The human intellect and will are not merely products of celestial causation, bodily humors, or external stimuli. Oresme’s opposition to astrological determinism belongs directly to his philosophy of mind. He does not deny all celestial influence on bodies, weather, or humoral dispositions, but he rejects the claim that human choices are fixed by the stars. His anti-astrological writings attack predictive and political astrology partly because they threaten rational agency.

The freedom argument is especially revealing. Oresme thinks the intellect is not purely passive. It receives intelligible species, habits, acts, and dispositions, but it also acts. God’s universal agency, external objects, and images in the imagination do not suffice to explain why a new act of understanding occurs. The intellect determines itself and can act when it wishes, absent impediment. This self-determining power underwrites praise, blame, deliberation, and moral responsibility.

5. Agent intellect, possible intellect, and intellectual activity

Oresme’s account of intellect is anti-Averroist, but it does not simply revert to an uncomplicated Thomism. He rejects the idea of one separate human intellect for all human beings. He also rejects the reduction of the possible intellect to the cogitative or recollective powers of the sensitive soul. Thinking occurs when intelligible species are received in the intellect, not merely when sensory or internal-sense powers operate.

On the agent intellect, Oresme’s most important move is to avoid multiplying intellects unnecessarily. The same human intellect can be considered under active and passive aspects. It is acted upon when it receives intelligible species, acts, habits, and intellectual dispositions; it acts when it understands, attends, abstracts, judges, and initiates intellectual operation. There is no need to posit a separate agent intellect as an external substance if the one human intellect can be both receptive and active in different respects.

This has direct consequences for philosophy of mind. Oresme’s intellect is not a passive container of mental representations. It is not merely a screen on which sensory images appear. It is a power capable of receiving, forming, judging, comparing, and self-activating. It depends on sensory and imaginative materials, but it is not exhausted by them.

His view also makes the mind individuated. Against Averroes, if there were only one intellect for all human beings, individual persons would not properly be the subjects of their acts of understanding. Oresme regards that as philosophically and ethically unacceptable. The intellect is intrinsic to the human difference; rationality belongs to this human being, not to a separate common intellect that merely uses this human being as an instrument.

This is one of the strongest parts of Oresme’s philosophy of mind. He wants all three of the following theses:

The intellect is immaterial.

The intellect belongs to an individual human person.

The intellect depends operationally on the body’s sensory and imaginative life.

His dual-form hylomorphism is designed to make all three claims coherent.

6. Perception: not passive reception, but fallible judgment

Oresme’s theory of perception is one of the most philosophically interesting parts of his psychology. French scholarship has rightly emphasized that he is not just repeating a standard Aristotelian-scholastic theory of species. Jean Celeyrette’s study of appearances and imaginations in Oresme shows that, for him, all knowledge begins from sense perceptions, but visual perception gives very few certainties. Appearances are furnished by both external and internal senses, and from appearances we try to know things; yet the route from appearance to truth is mediated by comparison, judgment, and often conjecture.

Oresme’s treatment of motion is especially revealing. Seeing that something is moving is not the same as having an infallible visual intuition of motion. Motion perception requires comparison over time. The virtus distinctiva, an interior discriminating power associated with common sense or internal sense, compares appearances; then intellect may judge. If the judgment is false, the error often lies not in the mere sensory reception but in the discourse or judgment built upon it.

Celeyrette’s analysis emphasizes that Oresme denies certain and evident experiential knowledge of motion. Reason and plausible conjecture may lead us to say that something is moved, but motion is not known intuitively with complete certainty; it is known abstractively and fallibly.

This gives Oresme a quasi-constructivist theory of perception. Perception is not a simple imprint of the world into the soul. It is an operation involving species, organs, comparison, internal sense, imagination, attention, and intellectual judgment. Modern scholars such as Christophe Grellard and José Filipe Silva have described Oresme’s perception theory as active, involving judgment, recognition, attention, habit, custom, and quasi-reasoning by internal sense.

The resulting philosophy of perception has several major claims.

First, sensation is bodily and organ-dependent.

Second, perception is structured by internal powers that compare, retain, and discriminate.

Third, error is often not mere sensory malfunction but misjudgment: the mind takes an appearance to indicate more than it warrants.

Fourth, cognition is probabilistic in many ordinary cases. We know the world through appearances, but appearances do not always carry certainty.

Fifth, intellect depends on sensory and imaginative mediation, but it can also evaluate that mediation.

This is one reason Oresme’s philosophy of mind is not simply dualist in the crude sense. The intellect may be immaterial, but human cognition is deeply embodied, temporal, comparative, and fallible.

7. Species, appearance, and the possibility of illusion

Oresme works within the perspectivist and Aristotelian framework in which species or likenesses mediate perception. But he exploits that framework to produce a sophisticated theory of illusion. Species can represent existent things, but they can also be involved in appearances of things that do not exist as they appear. Celeyrette notes Oresme’s use of cases where God could conserve species while annihilating the object, or otherwise intervene so that the appearance and the external state of affairs come apart.

These divine cases should not be dismissed as theological ornament. They are modal experiments. Oresme is asking: what follows about human cognition if the same appearance could be produced under different ontological conditions? The answer is that appearance does not automatically entail the external object’s exact current condition. This is not crude skepticism; it is an analysis of the limits of sensory certainty.

His thought experiments become even more striking in the disputed question on appearance. Oresme imagines cases where God continuously diminishes bodies and sense organs so that diminution is not perceived, or where Socrates is created and annihilated at intervals such that an observer perceives continuous existence. These scenarios are not ordinary empirical hypotheses. They are tests of what human perception can and cannot certify.

Celeyrette’s conclusion is subtle: Oresme multiplies imaginary cases that reveal illusions concerning size, time, and motion; sensory knowledge is abstractive and uncertain; yet this is not simply skepticism, because mathematical reason and disciplined imagination enlarge what the intellect can investigate.

This gives Oresme a distinctive epistemology of mind. The mind is not trapped in appearances, but it must work through them. The intellect does not receive certainty automatically from the senses; it tests, compares, abstracts, imagines possible cases, and uses mathematical reasoning to discipline conjecture. Divine power, in these arguments, marks the boundary of natural necessity and shows that appearance and reality are modally separable.

8. Imagination: error, science, and spiritual configuration

Oresme’s imagination is not merely a storehouse of phantasms. It has at least four functions.

First, imagination preserves and recombines sensory images.

Second, it supplies the phantasms on which intellectual abstraction depends.

Third, it can mislead, especially when internal sense, habit, passion, or bodily disorder corrupts appearance.

Fourth, it can be disciplined into mathematical, moral, and spiritual cognition.

The Spanish-language article on Job and contemplation is useful here because it draws attention to Oresme’s De configurationibus, especially the idea of configuratio spiritualis. In that text, Oresme distinguishes exterior and interior sense: exterior vision does not always retain the object, whereas interior sense can represent the object after its absence, like an impression remaining in wax after a seal has been removed. This means the intellect or soul may be “figured” correctly or incorrectly.

The same study explains that, for Oresme, thoughts and passions can make the soul rough, deformed, or impure; abstraction and turning toward the right object can remove such deformity. Job becomes an example of discernment because the removal of tormenting thoughts prepares the soul for contemplation. The role of image here is not merely epistemic but moral and spiritual: correct imagining is a condition for contemplating divine mysteries.

This is a crucial expansion of Oresme’s philosophy of mind. The imagination is not simply a lower faculty that intellect must overcome. It is the medium through which human beings receive, distort, compose, purify, and spiritually reconfigure appearances. It can produce illusions; it can also support mathematical idealization and contemplative ascent.

This also helps connect Oresme’s natural philosophy with his theology. In perception theory, imagination generates uncertainty and error. In mathematics and physics, imagination constructs possible cases that reason can analyze. In spiritual cognition, imagination can become morally configured toward divine contemplation. Oresme’s mind is therefore not just a knowing mechanism; it is a morally formable power.

9. Animal minds and the boundary of rationality

Oresme’s theory of animal mind is more generous than a simple denial of animal cognition, but less continuous than a modern evolutionary continuum. In discussing Alexander’s materialist position, Oresme acknowledges that brute animals can have complex cognitive capacities rooted in organs and sensitive powers. Zupko notes the striking point that Oresme considers arguments suggesting a continuum of animal cognition, including cases where monkeys or other animals might appear to reason if they could speak. But Oresme does not allow this to erase the metaphysical difference between sensitive cognition and human intellect.

Animal minds, for Oresme, are minds of the sensitive soul. They can perceive, discriminate, remember in a broad sense, desire, flee, pursue, and act with complex bodily organization. But they do not possess the immaterial intellect that grasps universals, reflects upon itself as intellect, deliberates freely in the full rational sense, or survives death.

Roudaut’s interpretation sharpens the point. Oresme’s non-human souls are not immaterial unities virtually present everywhere; they are extended, heterogeneous, organ-distributed, and deeply entangled with bodily configuration. This makes animal cognition real but material.

The boundary between animal and human is therefore not the boundary between “having experiences” and “having none.” It is the boundary between sensitive-organic cognition and immaterial intellectual cognition. Oresme’s animal psychology is not eliminativist. It is a material theory of sensitive mind.

10. Will, freedom, attention, and moral psychology

Oresme’s philosophy of mind includes will and freedom, not only cognition. The intellect can act when it wishes, and the human being is not fully explained by external causal chains. This is one reason he opposes astrological determinism. The stars may influence bodies, humors, weather, and broad natural conditions, but they do not determine rational choice. The Stanford article notes that Oresme attacks astrology on both religious and scientific grounds and is especially concerned with its political danger for princes and rulers.

In the De anima material, Oresme argues that God’s universal agency together with natural images or external objects is not sufficient to explain a new intellectual act. The intellect must itself be a causal source. Otherwise, praise and blame would be undermined.

This gives Oresme a theory of mental agency. The mind is not only receptive but active. Attention, judgment, abstraction, intellectual self-motion, and will belong together. The will is not an isolated faculty floating above cognition; it operates in a mind whose perceptual and imaginative materials may be clear, distorted, habitual, passionate, or pathologically disturbed.

This also explains why mental disorders matter for his philosophy of mind. If bodily disorder can disturb fantasy, imagination, and intellectual operation, then freedom is not simply an all-or-nothing metaphysical possession. It has conditions of exercise. A human being may retain the basic potencies of soul while intellect and will fail to act normally because the bodily-sensitive basis is damaged.

11. Mental disorder, marvels, and natural explanation

Oresme’s De causis mirabilium is essential for understanding his philosophy of mind because it treats extraordinary experiences, illusions, marvels, and false explanations. The Brepols description of the critical edition and translation emphasizes that Oresme argues against attributing marvels too quickly to celestial influence, demons, or God; many marvels result from misperceptions, errors of sensation, or faulty reasoning, and can be explained naturally.

This does not make Oresme a secular reductionist in the modern sense. He does not deny God, demons, angels, or miracles. Rather, he rejects careless explanation. To say “God did it,” “demons did it,” or “the stars did it” without necessity is, for him, bad natural philosophy. The mind’s vulnerability to misperception and faulty inference is itself a natural cause of many alleged marvels.

The same pattern appears in late medieval discussions of mental disorder. Vesa Hirvonen’s article on Witelo, Oresme, and Gerson states that these authors usually understand madness as having bodily, humoral, or organic origins, while still allowing that divine or demonic causes may stand behind immediate causes. In such cases, sensory fantasy is disturbed, intellect does not act normally, and the will lacks freedom; but the person has not lost the basic parts or potencies of the soul.

This is philosophically rich. Mental illness does not destroy the soul’s essence. It impairs the conditions under which imagination, intellect, and will operate. Oresme therefore has something like a layered psychopathology:

Bodily or humoral disorder can affect sensory organs and internal fantasy.

Disturbed fantasy can present false or disordered appearances.

The intellect, dependent on phantasms for ordinary operation, may fail to judge normally.

The will, dependent on rational apprehension and normal psychic operation, may lack freedom in the relevant act.

Yet the person remains human because the fundamental potencies of the soul remain.

This fits perfectly with Oresme’s dual-form psychology. Human intellect is immaterial, but its ordinary exercise is mediated by bodily-sensitive and imaginative conditions. Damage those conditions, and the intellect’s operation is impeded without being metaphysically annihilated.

12. God’s mind and divine agency in Oresme’s philosophy of mind

Oresme does not, in the materials surveyed here, provide a full faculty psychology of God’s intellect comparable to a scholastic treatise on divine ideas. But God is indispensable to his philosophy of mind in at least five ways.

First, God is the creator of the human intellective soul. Oresme denies that the intellective soul is educed from matter. It is created, immaterial, unextended, and not corruptible by bodily corruption. This establishes the intellect’s metaphysical difference from material sensitive powers.

Second, God is the universal causal background of intellectual action, but not a substitute for the intellect’s own agency. Oresme explicitly rejects the idea that God’s universal agency plus external images or objects suffices to explain a new act of understanding. The human intellect must also act. This is a sophisticated concurrence model: divine causality is real, but it does not erase created intellectual self-determination.

Third, God functions as a modal operator in Oresme’s epistemology. When Oresme imagines God conserving species while annihilating the object, or creating and annihilating Socrates in such a way that continuous existence appears to be perceived, he is not using God as an arbitrary plot device. He is using divine omnipotence to explore the modal gap between appearance and reality.

Fourth, God is tied to eternity. The Stanford article reports that Oresme defines the duration of things without succession as eternity and identifies eternity with God himself. This matters for philosophy of mind because the human intellect’s grasp of eternal truths and separate substances is one of the reasons Oresme treats it as immaterial; divine eternity is the ultimate horizon of non-successive being.

Fifth, God’s justice supports the moral argument for the soul’s survival. Oresme argues that if the human soul did not survive death, perfect justice would be lacking in the universe. Again, this is not merely devotional. It connects moral psychology, freedom, responsibility, and immortality. A world in which rational agents deliberate, merit, err, and suffer unjustly requires a metaphysical completion beyond bodily death.

So, if we ask what “the mind of God” contributes to Oresme’s philosophy of mind, the answer is not that God is simply another mind alongside ours. God is the uncreated source of intellective souls, the universal cause whose action does not cancel created agency, the modal ground of thought experiments about appearance, the eternal horizon of intellectual objects, and the moral guarantor of justice.

That is a very significant role. It should not be dismissed as a theological distraction.

13. Celestial intelligences, angels, and separate minds

Oresme’s relevant philosophical texts often speak of “intelligences” rather than developing a full angelology. In medieval cosmological usage, these celestial intelligences are closely related to what a theological register would call angelic minds: immaterial created intellects associated with the motion or governance of celestial spheres. For Oresme’s philosophy of mind, they are important because they provide a comparison class: minds that are immaterial and separate, but causally related to a body or sphere in a way different from human embodiment.

In the Averroist model Oresme discusses, the human possible intellect is separate and is “appropriated” to human bodies somewhat as celestial intelligences are related to celestial spheres. Those intelligences perform their primary act by intellectual reflection and their secondary act by moving the spheres. Oresme uses the analogy but rejects its application to human intellect. Human beings are generated and corrupted; human understanding depends on bodily senses and phantasms; and rational agency belongs to individual persons.

Oresme also distinguishes the composition of separate substances from the composition of material things. Separate intelligences do not have matter/form composition in the same sense as bodies. This matters because it prevents a crude analogy: the human intellect is immaterial, but the human being is not simply an angel using a body. The human intellect is the substantial form of a body, while celestial intelligences are separate substances associated with spheres in another mode.

The celestial intelligence analogy also helps Oresme argue that multiple formal or intellectual principles associated with one subject or sphere are at least conceivable. He notes that multiple intelligences may be connected with the same celestial sphere, though he is careful not to claim exact equivalence with the human case.

The philosophical upshot is this: angelic or celestial minds help Oresme map the space between material cognition and divine intellect. They are created immaterial intellects that do not operate through bodily organs in the way human beings do. But precisely for that reason, they cannot be used to explain human intellect by simple assimilation. Human intellect is lower than angelic intellect in its dependence on phantasms, but higher than animal cognition in its immateriality, universality, and freedom.

14. Immortality and the “perpetual” soul

Oresme distinguishes eternity and perpetuity. A being can be perpetual by having a beginning but no end; eternity strictly lacks both beginning and end. The human intellective soul is not eternal in the divine sense, because it is created. But it may be perpetual: it begins, yet survives bodily death.

His arguments for immortality are not presented as strict demonstrations. They are persuasive convergences. The intellect is immaterial and unextended; what is immaterial and indivisible is not corrupted as bodies are. The human being desires perpetual happiness. The intellect can know eternal and separate substances. Moral justice requires completion beyond the present life.

This is another point where Oresme’s theology and philosophy are inseparable without being indistinguishable. Faith gives certainty, but natural philosophy and moral reasoning provide plausible support. Oresme compares positions associated with Averroes, Alexander, Plato, and Christian faith, and he treats the question as one where natural reason can weigh probabilities even when it cannot fully demonstrate the conclusion.

From a philosophy-of-mind standpoint, immortality follows from the intellect’s mode of being. The sensitive soul perishes because it is material and extended. The intellective soul remains because it is not generated from matter and is not corrupted with matter. The dead human’s intellectual survival is not the survival of the whole embodied cognitive system; it is the survival of the immaterial intellective form.

That creates a tension. If ordinary human thought depends on phantasms and bodily senses, what is the operation of the separated soul after death? Oresme’s materials, at least as represented in the sources surveyed, do not fully resolve that tension. But the tension is not accidental. It is the same tension that structures his entire philosophy of mind: the human intellect is immaterial, yet human cognition is embodied.

15. Anti-superstition, astrology, and the autonomy of mind

Oresme’s anti-astrological writings should be included in his philosophy of mind because astrology threatens the autonomy of judgment and will. He does not deny that heavenly bodies may have natural effects. But he attacks judicial astrology, divination, and predictive claims about human fortune or political action. The Stanford article notes that he opposed astrology on religious and scientific grounds, argued that celestial motions are probably incommensurable and therefore not precisely predictable in the way astrologers require, and warned that astrology was especially dangerous for princes and rulers.

This position is continuous with his broader treatment of marvels. Oresme is trying to discipline explanation. Do not attribute ordinary or misunderstood phenomena to demons, stars, or divine intervention when sensory error, faulty reasoning, bodily causes, or natural processes suffice. But do not infer from that methodological rule that God, angels, demons, or miracles are unreal. The correct principle is explanatory restraint, not metaphysical reductionism.

In terms of philosophy of mind, this gives Oresme a theory of rational independence. The mind is vulnerable to appearances, passions, humors, bad habits, and social superstition. It can be misled by false patterns and by overconfident inference. But the intellect’s task is to resist those distortions through reasoned judgment, mathematical analysis, and disciplined attention to natural causes.

16. The extracted system: Oresme’s philosophy of mind in propositions

Here is the most compact systematic reconstruction.

First, soul is not univocal across living beings. Plant, animal, human, angelic, and divine cases are ordered but not reducible to one model. Oresme’s philosophy of mind is pluralist and hierarchical.

Second, animal mind is material, extended, and organ-localized. Sensitive powers are not merely names; they are real formal-organic structures. Seeing belongs to the visual organ, hearing to the auditory organ, and so forth. The animal soul is a heterogeneous material form distributed through bodily powers.

Third, human beings have two substantial forms: a material sensitive form and an immaterial intellective form. This is Oresme’s central solution to the problem of embodied intellect. The sensitive form accounts for bodily life and perception; the intellective form accounts for universal, reflective, free, and potentially immortal cognition.

Fourth, the human intellect is immaterial but not Averroistically separate. It inheres in the individual human being as a substantial form, while being unextended and not educed from matter. It is not one common intellect shared by all human beings.

Fifth, human cognition is embodied in operation. The intellect depends on senses, phantasms, internal sense, and imagination. This dependence is operational rather than reductive: bodily conditions are needed for ordinary thinking, but they do not make intellect a bodily organ.

Sixth, perception is active, comparative, and fallible. Sensory appearances require internal discrimination and intellectual judgment. Error often arises when the mind over-interprets appearances. Motion, magnitude, and continuity are especially vulnerable to misperception and conjecture.

Seventh, imagination is central. It mediates abstraction, produces illusions, supports mathematical and physical thought experiments, and can be morally or spiritually configured. It is not a disposable lower faculty; it is the hinge between body, intellect, and contemplative life.

Eighth, the intellect is both receptive and active. It receives species and habits, but it also initiates acts of understanding. The same intellect can be active and passive in different respects, so one need not posit a separate agent intellect as an external substance.

Ninth, freedom is a mark of intellective mind. The human mind is not determined by stars, bodily stimuli, or divine concurrence alone. It can deliberate, attend, judge, and initiate action; otherwise praise and blame would collapse.

Tenth, mental disorder shows embodied dependence without eliminating the soul. Bodily or humoral disturbance can disorder fantasy, impede intellect, and remove freedom in particular acts; but the person does not lose the basic potencies of soul.

Eleventh, God is philosophically internal to the system. God creates the intellective soul, supplies universal causality without erasing human agency, defines the modal horizon of epistemological thought experiments, and grounds the moral argument for immortality.

Twelfth, angelic or celestial intelligences are also philosophically internal. They show what separate created minds are like and clarify why human intellect cannot be understood either as merely animal or as simply angelic.

17. Major tensions and unresolved problems

The first tension is the unity of the human being. Oresme wants two substantial forms but one human person. He argues that the forms are of different kinds and that the sensitive form disposes the body for the intellective form, but the metaphysical unity remains delicate. This is the price of avoiding both reductive materialism and Averroist separation.

The second tension is embodiment versus immortality. If ordinary human thinking depends on phantasms, what does the separated intellective soul do after death? Oresme argues for survival, but the sources surveyed give more detail about why the soul survives than about the phenomenology or operation of separated human cognition.

The third tension is between naturalistic explanation and theological possibility. Oresme frequently uses God’s power, divine justice, and celestial intelligences in philosophical arguments, while also warning against over-attributing phenomena to God, demons, or stars. This is not inconsistency. It is a distinction between ultimate metaphysical causes and proximate natural explanations. But it requires discipline: God is not to be invoked lazily, yet divine power remains philosophically decisive.

The fourth tension concerns animal cognition. Oresme grants rich sensitive capacities to animals but denies them immaterial intellect. His view can look generous when compared with crude denials of animal mind, but restrictive when compared with modern continuist accounts. The recent reductionist reading of his animal psychology intensifies this tension by making non-human soul-body distinction very thin.

The fifth tension is epistemological. Oresme’s theory of perception threatens skepticism because appearances can be detached from reality, motion is not certainly known by sense, and divine possibilities reveal the limits of intuition. Yet he does not surrender to skepticism. He turns instead to probable reasoning, mathematical imagination, and disciplined abstraction.

Conclusion

Oresme’s philosophy of mind is best understood as a non-Cartesian dualism of human intellect embedded within a broader natural philosophy of embodied cognition. He is dualist about the human being because he posits both a material sensitive form and an immaterial intellective form. But he is not dualist in a way that neglects the body. Perception, imagination, fantasy, attention, bodily disorder, sense organs, humors, and internal judgment are indispensable to ordinary human cognition.

He is also not a simple materialist about animals, though his animal psychology is strongly embodied and may approach a reduction of sensitive powers to bodily configurations. Nor is he a simple spiritualist about human beings, because the intellect’s operations depend on sensory and imaginative mediation. Nor is he a secular naturalist, because God, divine justice, and celestial intelligences are integral to the metaphysical architecture. But he is methodologically naturalistic in his explanations of marvels, illusions, mental disorders, and superstition.

The most comprehensive reconstruction is therefore this: Oresme offers a layered theory of mind in which material sensitive cognition, human embodied intellect, separate angelic intelligence, and divine causality occupy distinct but related levels. Human mind is the most philosophically dramatic case because it stands at the intersection: bodily enough to depend on phantasms, immaterial enough to know universals, active enough to be free, fragile enough to be deceived, and enduring enough to survive the body.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
